author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
Nature of Human Righteousness and Perfection.
title: Nature of Human Righteousness and Perfection.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For from the place in which he undertook to say these things, he thus began, “Beware of dogs, beware of evil workers, beware of the concision.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For we are the circumcision, who serve God in the Spirit,”—or, as some codices have it, “who serve God the Spirit,” or “the Spirit of God,”—“and glory in Christ Jesus, and have no confidence in the flesh.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Here it is manifest that he is speaking against the Jews, who, observing the law carnally, and going about to establish their own righteousness, were slain by the letter, and not made alive by the Spirit, and gloried in themselves while the apostles and all the children of the promise were glorying in Christ.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Then he added, “Although I may have confidence in the flesh.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: If any one else thinks that he has confidence in the flesh, I more.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And enumerating all things which have glory according to the flesh, he ended at that point where he says, “According to the righteousness which is in the law, blameless.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And when he had said that he regarded all these things as altogether loss and disadvantage and dung that he might gain Christ, he added the passage which I am treating, “And be found in Him, not having my own righteousness, but that which is by the faith of Christ, which is from God.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: He confessed that he had not yet received the perfection of this righteousness, which will not be except in that excellent knowledge of Christ, on account of which he said that all things were loss to him; and he confessed, therefore, that he was not yet perfect.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: “But I follow on,” said he, “if I may apprehend that in which I also am apprehended of Christ Jesus.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: “I may apprehend that in which I also am apprehended,” is much the same as, “I may know, even as I also am known.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: “Brethren,” says he, “I count not myself to have apprehended: but one thing, forgetting those things which are behind, and reaching forward to those which are before, I follow on according to the purpose for the reward of the supreme calling of God in Christ Jesus.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: The order of the words is, “But one thing I follow.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Of which one thing the Lord also is well understood to have admonished Martha, where he says, “Martha, Martha, thou art careful and troubled about many things: but one thing is needful.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: The apostle, wishing to apprehend this as if set in the way, said that he followed on to the reward of the high calling of God in Christ Jesus.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For who can delay when he would apprehend that which he declares that he is following, that he shall then have a righteousness equal to the righteousness of the holy angels, none of whom, of course, does any messenger of Satan buffet lest he should be lifted up with the greatness of his revelations?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Then, admonishing those who might think themselves already perfect with the fulness of that righteousness, he says, “Let as many of us, therefore, as are perfect, be thus minded.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: As if he should say, If, according to the capacity of mortal man for the little measure of this life, we are perfect, let us understand that it also belongs to that perfection that we perceive that we are not yet perfected in that angelical righteousness which we shall have in the manifestation of Christ.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: “And if in anything,” he said, “ye be otherwise minded, God shall also reveal even this unto you.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: How, save to those that are walking and advancing in the way of the faith, until that wandering be finished and they come to the actual vision?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Whence following on, he added, “Nevertheless, whereunto we have already attained, let us walk therein.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Then he concludes that they should be bewared of, concerning whom this passage treated at its beginning.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: “Brethren, be imitators of me, and mark them which so walk as ye have our example.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For many walk, of whom I have spoken often, and now tell you even weeping, whose end is destruction,” and the rest.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: These are the very ones of whom, in the beginning, he had said, “Beware of dogs, beware of evil workers,” and what follows.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Therefore all are enemies of the cross of Christ who, going about to establish their own righteousness, which is of the law,—that is, where only the letter commands, and the Spirit does not fulfil,—are not subject to the law of God.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For if they who are of the law be heirs, faith is made an empty thing.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: “If righteousness is by the law, then Christ has died in vain: then is the offence of the cross done away.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And thus those are enemies of the cross of Christ who say that righteousness is by the law, to which it belongs to command, not to assist.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But the grace of God through Jesus Christ the Lord in the Holy Spirit helpeth our infirmity.
